content:
6 min read
Just now

--

We designed our technologies to remove unnecessary effort. The harder question is whether, in doing so, we’ve also begun removing the experiences that build capable human beings.
A teenager waits three seconds for a webpage to load and sighs.
An office worker follows GPS through streets they already know.
A student opens an AI assistant before even attempting the first sentence of an essay.
None of these moments feels remarkable. They’re ordinary. Sensible, even. If something can be done with less effort, why wouldn’t we choose that path?
That instinct has shaped far more than our daily habits.
Over time, countless small improvements have made life smoother. We skip lines. We get directions instantly. We automate work that once consumed entire afternoons. Each innovation solves a real problem, and few of us would willingly give them up.
But something else has happened along the way.
Taken together, these improvements have created a world that increasingly treats friction as something to eliminate. Not because anyone intended it, but because that’s what the system rewards.
Which raises a different question.
The issue isn’t whether convenience makes life better.
It’s what happens to people when the systems around them are built to make almost everything easier.
Every System Produces More Than Its Intended Outcome
A transportation system doesn’t only move people.
A school doesn’t only teach.
A marketplace doesn’t simply exchange goods.
Every system rewards certain behaviors, discourages others, and, often without anyone noticing, shapes the people who live inside it.
That’s why optimization deserves more attention than it usually gets.
When a company redesigns a product to make it easier to use, it’s responding to customers.
When engineers remove unnecessary steps, they’re improving the experience.
When governments digitize services, they’re reducing wasted time.
When artificial intelligence automates repetitive work, it frees people for other tasks.
Each decision makes sense on its own.
The interesting part only appears when we stop looking at the individual improvements and start looking at the pattern they create together.
Seen from that distance, something changes.
Society itself begins optimizing for effortlessness.
Difficulty starts feeling like poor design instead of a normal part of learning. Waiting feels broken. Confusion feels unacceptable. Resistance becomes something to avoid rather than something to understand.
Nobody planned for this.
It emerged from thousands of reasonable decisions interacting over time.
Local Success Can Produce Systemic Failure
Systems thinking often begins with a simple observation.
People can improve their own part of a system while unintentionally making the whole system worse.
A designer removes three clicks.
An engineer speeds up an application.
A retailer promises delivery by tomorrow.
A government simplifies permit applications.
Every one of those improvements succeeds.
Yet together they reinforce the same expectation-that tomorrow should always demand less effort than today.
That’s where the trade-off begins to appear.
The problem isn’t that these optimizations fail.
It’s that every optimization strengthens one value while quietly pushing another into the background.
Every Optimization Has a Hidden Cost
Think about a city built almost entirely around cars.
Travel becomes easier.
But neighborhoods often become quieter because fewer people walk, stop, or meet one another.
The design succeeds at what it was built to do.
The cost simply shows up somewhere else.
The same pattern repeats across many systems.
An education system focused on standardized testing can narrow learning.
A workplace driven entirely by measurable productivity may leave less room for experimentation.
Technology follows the same logic.
Speed can come at the expense of reflection.
Automation can reduce opportunities to practice.
Prediction can crowd out exploration.
Convenience can slowly erase the kinds of challenges that develop patience, judgment, resilience, and mastery.
None of those outcomes is intentional.
They’re simply what every optimization leaves behind when one goal consistently outweighs the others.
Not All Friction Is Waste
This matters because we often treat all friction as though it’s the same.
It isn’t.
Some friction serves no purpose.
Broken software.
Confusing bureaucracy.
Poorly designed forms.
Long waits created by inefficient systems.
Removing those obstacles makes life better.
Other forms of friction are different.
Learning another language.
Practicing an instrument.
Building a business.
Repairing a damaged relationship.
Writing something difficult until it finally becomes clear.
These experiences are challenging, but their difficulty isn’t a design flaw.
It’s where the learning happens.
A pianist improves because mistakes reveal what still needs work.
An athlete grows stronger because muscles respond to resistance.
A writer develops judgment through revision, not inspiration alone.
The struggle isn’t separate from the outcome.
It’s part of the mechanism that produces it.
That’s developmental friction.
Its role isn’t to slow us down.
Its role is to change us.
Capability Is Built Through Repetition
Most human abilities don’t disappear all at once.
They fade quietly.
Every time technology makes a decision on our behalf, that’s one less decision we practice making ourselves.
Every automation removes another opportunity to exercise a skill.
No single shortcut seems important.
The accumulation is.
Less effort leads to less practice.
Less practice weakens capability.
As capability declines, dependence grows. That dependence creates demand for even more automation, and the cycle begins reinforcing itself.
You could think of this as capability debt.
Software developers know technical debt well. A shortcut saves time today but creates work later.
Capability works much the same way.
Convenience delivers immediate benefits.
But when meaningful practice disappears, so do the abilities that practice once sustained.
The debt often remains invisible until life asks us to do something we’ve quietly stopped learning how to do.
The issue isn’t technology.
It’s forgetting that skills survive only when they’re used.
Convenience Changes Expectations
Systems don’t just change what we do.
They change what feels normal.
A five-second delay once seemed astonishingly fast.
Now it feels slow.
Human nature didn’t suddenly change.
Our expectations did.
Each improvement becomes the new baseline. As that baseline shifts, patience has fewer chances to develop because the world asks less of it.
Eventually impatience no longer feels learned.
It simply feels natural.
A Different Standard for Progress
At its core, this isn’t really an argument about technology.
It’s an argument about human development.
If progress means removing every form of effort, then friction is always a problem.
But much of human history points in another direction.
For centuries, philosophers argued that character grows through repeated action.
Modern psychology reaches a remarkably similar conclusion.
People become more capable by engaging challenges that stretch them without overwhelming them.
Competence is practiced.
Wisdom is accumulated.
Resilience is earned.
So perhaps the better question isn’t whether machines perform certain tasks better than humans.
Often they do.
The more important question is what disappears when those tasks disappear.
Do we lose only effort?
Or do we also lose opportunities to develop judgment, creativity, responsibility, and resilience?
Sometimes we don’t.
Sometimes we do.
Learning to tell the difference may become one of the defining challenges of this century.
Designing Systems That Build Better Humans
Technology isn’t destiny.
Neither are markets.
Neither is artificial intelligence.
Systems are human creations, which means they can always be redesigned.
Convenience remains one of humanity’s great achievements.
But its purpose was never to remove every form of effort.
It was to eliminate unnecessary effort so that people could spend more of themselves on work that actually matters.
That distinction changes how we measure progress.
Success isn’t simply a world where people do less.
It’s a world where the systems we build leave people more capable than they were before.
Because every tool changes the people who use it.
The real challenge, then, isn’t deciding how to eliminate friction.
It’s deciding which kinds of friction should disappear-and which are too valuable to lose because they’re the very experiences through which wisdom, character, and excellence are formed.
Key Takeaways

• Systems shape people by rewarding certain behaviors repeatedly over time.

• Local improvements can create unintended societal consequences when they reinforce the same incentive.

• Developmental friction is valuable because it provides the feedback necessary for learning and growth.

• Convenience can create “capability debt” when it replaces meaningful practice instead of eliminating meaningless work.

• Progress should be judged not only by efficiency but by whether our systems cultivate stronger, wiser, and more capable human beings.
Credits
Inspired by: The Convenience Trap: How Ease Became Our Master by EE
